metrics.narrative: Business problem and qualitative pain are clearly articulated; quantified ROI is implied but not yet formally modeled. Utchal stated on 20 May that the procurement team spends '50 to 60% of our time in transactions' (follow-ups, approvals, chasing information), which he described as 'not being very efficient for the procurement organization' and making procurement 'more of an admin job' rather than a strategic role focused on cost savings and long-term vendor partnerships. He identified lack of historical spend visibility ('procurement team is working blindsided'), manual vendor onboarding with multiple handoffs, manual RFP/contract creation, and no automated PO generation as the core operational drags. On the value case, Utchal proposed a conservative 4-5% savings target on the in-scope indirect spend (~10 lakh crore, so 40,000-50,000 crore potential savings floor), noting that 'if we take a conservative approach... that's a huge number for us' and acknowledged the industry benchmark is 7-8%. He also flagged spend leakage from duplicate vendors, tail spend, and lack of contract utilization tracking. However, no formal ROI workshop or quantified business case has been conducted yet. The custom demo on 3 July and any follow-on commercial discussions will be the venue to model time savings, cost avoidance, and process acceleration. Gap: no CFO or finance stakeholder engaged to validate the savings model or approve budget.

champion.narrative: Utchal Barbara (Head of Procurement, title per SF contact) is a confirmed and developing champion. He is the operational advocate driving the evaluation, has articulated clear pain and business case, requested detailed proof points and FMCG use cases for internal selling, and is actively coordinating next sessions with senior stakeholders (CDO, Procurement Head, deal owner's manager). Evidence of advocacy: (1) Utchal proactively requested the discovery call and F2F meeting, submitted detailed use-case requirements (9-10 items) ahead of the clarification call, and is scheduling the custom demo with IT team participation; (2) He stated on 20 May 'I know Zycus... I have had friends who worked in Zycus earlier' (prior awareness, positive signal) and responded positively to the Merlin Teams demo ('good to know that'); (3) He is building the business case for internal stakeholders, explicitly asking Zycus to 'come out with FMCG use cases... when I'm going to go to a program sponsor he should be able to see that this is the spend analysis we did in XYZ organization, before we did that this is what we were doing, once we implemented this is the amount of savings which had come' — this is classic champion behavior (internal selling); (4) He is coordinating access to power: requested next session include 'the deal owner's manager and Chief Digital Officer' and confirmed CDO as 'main sponsor of the project'; (5) He is setting realistic expectations and surfacing blockers early ('some of the requirements we cant deliver, will need clarity on that call' per 23 June Next_Step — a champion surfaces obstacles rather than hiding them). Strength: developing. Utchal has operational authority and is an active advocate, but he does not control the commercial decision (EB unmapped) and has not yet explicitly positioned Zycus as preferred vs Ariba/GEP/Ivalua. His hands-on Ariba implementation experience (J&J 17-country rollout) is a double-edged signal: he knows what good looks like and has high credibility internally, but Ariba has incumbent credibility and familiarity. On 20 May he stated 'I am discussing with you, then... I will say you guys I have the RFP... I am also looking at other tools,' confirming he is running a competitive process and has not yet advocated for sole-source Zycus. Champion strength will be tested on the 3 July demo: if Zycus resolves the functional gap and activates the AI/Merlin wedge compellingly, Utchal can become a strong champion; if the gap is unresolved or Ariba/GEP can deliver what Zycus cannot, his advocacy may cool. Current engagement: active (demo scheduled 3 July, last spoke 10 June, responsive to scheduling). Risk: champion has procurement authority but not commercial/budget authority, so his advocacy alone cannot close the deal without EB engagement. Next move: on 3 July, deepen the champion relationship by resolving the functional gap, delivering compelling FMCG proof points, and enlisting Utchal to map the EB and coach Zycus on the internal approval/political landscape.

competition.narrative: Competitive landscape is confirmed and active, with SAP Ariba as the primary threat. Utchal stated on 20 May 'I know how Zycus works, GEP, iValua, Ariba... I have myself done implementation across the globe... Ariba p2p implementation in Philippines with Johnson and Johnson, Ariba e-market rollout across Latin America for Johnson and Johnson 17 countries,' confirming hands-on Ariba implementation experience and familiarity with GEP and Ivalua. He also confirmed parallel evaluation: 'I am also looking at other tools... we are going to be looking at the other tools... post that we wanna take a decision very very soon.' No competitor is named as active finalist yet (deal is early Qualified stage, no formal RFP or shortlist), but buyer's Ariba experience gives Ariba incumbent credibility and low implementation-risk perception. Ariba was also mentioned in the 20 May AI notes as a known platform from prior implementations. Wedge: Zycus positioned on 20 May as differentiated via (1) organic end-to-end S2P suite on single codebase vs competitors' bolt-on acquisitions ('when you talk about our competitors they have mostly acquired a lot of companies... whenever there's integration there's a leakage of value and the platform is not able to perform to the optimal... with Zycus the entire suite is built organically on one line of code'), (2) AI-native platform ('the core of our platform is built on AI... we are AI native... we started including AI from 2000... we were the first one to implement agentic AI'), and (3) conversational AI in Microsoft Teams (Merlin intake agent demoed, buyer responded 'good to know that'). Buyer has not yet confirmed Zycus superiority or positioned Zycus as preferred. If Zycus cannot resolve the functional gap flagged 23 June ('some of the requirements we cant deliver') or if Ariba/GEP can deliver cross-plant stock visibility, fast article-code creation, or other buyer-critical requirements that Zycus cannot, Ariba wins on familiarity + known implementation risk. How we win: (1) Resolve the functional gap on 3 July demo with a credible solution or acceptable trade-off/workaround; (2) Activate the AI/Merlin wedge compellingly (show conversational AI driving adoption and time savings vs traditional ERP-style workflows); (3) Deliver strong FMCG proof points (HUL, ITC Foods) with quantified before/after savings that Utchal can take to the CDO and program sponsor; (4) Prove fast implementation timeline that meets 'this year' urgency; (5) Map and engage the EB to validate budget/commercial authority and neutralize Ariba's incumbent advantage. Current threat level: Ariba high (hands-on buyer experience, incumbent credibility, active parallel evaluation); GEP medium (named, buyer familiar, but no evidence of active engagement); Ivalua medium (named, buyer familiar, but no evidence of active engagement).

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is clearly identified and articulated by Utchal (Head of Procurement, operational champion) on the 20 May discovery call and reinforced on the 10 June clarification call. The pain owner is the procurement organization (Utchal and his team), and the pain is both operational (time/effort waste on manual transactions, lack of process automation) and strategic (no spend visibility, no data-driven decision-making, inability to shift from admin/transactional work to strategic sourcing and cost savings). Specific pain points: (1) 50-60% of procurement team time consumed by manual follow-ups, chasing approvals, and transactional tasks ('our team spends so much of time in transactions... getting blocked almost 50 to 60% of our time... that's not being very efficient'); (2) Procurement team is 'working blindsided' with no historical spend data, no contract status visibility, no vendor pricing history ('we do not have access to data as to what was bought last year at what price... if something was bought last year and I was not there in the organization then I don't even know as to what did the previous buyer buy it at what price'); (3) Manual vendor onboarding with email-based handoffs, multiple approval loops, and human touchpoints ('vendor sends documents, procurement forwards to another team, that team uploads it, then it goes to various levels of approval... a lot of human touch points'); (4) Manual RFP/sourcing process with no centralized bid comparison or real-time status tracking ('I create my templates, everybody creates their own templates, we send it to suppliers, suppliers respond back, we are done with negotiation, then we send it to procurement head saying these are the various bids, this is the l1 supplier, can you give us an approval, approval comes under mail'); (5) No automated PO creation after sourcing approval ('once sourcing is done, approval came, then again we have PRs created for Capex, PO creation is manual, PO creation needs to go through different levels of approval even though entire sourcing process has already been approved'); (6) No proactive budget checks, leading to completing entire sourcing cycles only to discover no budget is available ('we complete the entire sourcing process and then after that we came to note that budget is not even there'); (7) Spend leakage from duplicate vendors, tail spend, and no contract utilization tracking (buyer acknowledged 4-5% savings opportunity floor from fixing this). The pain is acute: Utchal described it as making procurement 'more of an admin job' when 'in today's world it's more of a strategy role... your KRAs are how much cost saving you're doing, long term partnership with vendors,' and he stated urgency to fix it ('we wanna take a decision very very soon... implementation should be this year itself'). Pain is confirmed across every category Zycus can address (spend analytics, supplier onboarding, sourcing, contract lifecycle, PR-to-PO, invoice automation). No quantified financial impact per pain point yet (e.g. cost of delay, error rate, maverick spend %), but qualitative pain is strong and the conservative 4-5% savings target implies the pain owner (procurement leadership) has C-level visibility and sponsorship (CDO as program sponsor).

economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer is unmapped and unengaged. The Chief Digital Officer (CDO) was named on 20 May as 'the main sponsor of the project' and attended the F2F meeting on 29 May where 'they liked the Zycus capabilities,' but the CDO's role is program sponsor, not confirmed Economic Buyer. No commercial, pricing, or budget authority conversation has occurred. On a $100k S2P transformation for Reliance Industries (annual spend ~20 lakh crore), the Economic Buyer will be C-level (likely CFO, CPO, or CXO with P&L ownership), and multi-level approvals are standard for an enterprise of this scale. Utchal (Head of Procurement, champion) is the operational advocate but does not control the commercial decision. The account has zero OpportunityContactRole records, so no EB, DM, or Executive Sponsor is formally mapped. The Next_Step log references a 'Procurement Head' (title only, not named) who attended the 29 May F2F and an 'AVP of sourcing' whom Utchal wanted to include in the next session (requested 20 May), but neither has appeared on a recorded call and neither is the EB. On 20 May Utchal confirmed 'multiple approvals' and 'various levels of approval' for POs and projects, implying a multi-stakeholder decision process, but the approval chain and budget authority are not yet mapped. This is the single highest risk to the deal: without EB identification and engagement before commercials, the forecast is indefensible and the deal cannot progress beyond demo. Next move: use the 3 July demo (where CDO and deal owner's manager are expected) to map the EB and confirm the approval/budget process.

decision_process.narrative: Decision process is partially visible but not yet formalized. Utchal (Head of Procurement, operational champion) is driving the evaluation and stated on 20 May that he is 'discussing with you, then in between one of these days I will say you guys I have the RFP... or I may not give a formal RFP but I have already started engaging with vendors.' He confirmed parallel vendor evaluation in progress and that speed matters: 'we are going to be looking at the other tools... post that we wanna take a decision very very soon.' The process to date: discovery call 20 May with Utchal → F2F meeting 29 May with CDO (program sponsor) and Procurement Head (positive reception) → use-case requirements submitted by buyer → clarification call 10 June → custom demo originally scheduled 29 June, rescheduled to 3 July due to buyer IT team unavailability. Utchal requested on 20 May that the next session include 'the deal owner's manager and Chief Digital Officer' and that Zycus 'come out with FMCG use cases... this is what we have resolved in ITC foods or Hindustan Unilever... before we did that this is what we were doing, once we implemented this is the amount of savings which had come... so this is something when I'm going to go to a program sponsor he should be able to see that.' This indicates a multi-stakeholder approval: operational champion (Utchal) validates fit and builds the business case → program sponsor (CDO) and senior procurement leadership (Procurement Head, deal owner's manager) review use cases and approve shortlist → Economic Buyer (unmapped, likely CFO/CPO/CXO) approves commercial and budget. Utchal confirmed on 20 May that approvals go through 'various levels' and that 'multiple approvals' are standard. However, the formal approval chain, decision timeline, and RFP path (formal RFP vs parallel demos + commercial proposals) are not yet locked. Competitor engagement is live: Utchal has hands-on Ariba implementation experience (J&J 17-country rollout) and stated he 'knows how Ariba works, GEP, iValua,' so parallel evaluation is credible. Next gate: 3 July custom demo where functional gap ('some of the requirements we cant deliver') must be resolved and EB/approval chain must be mapped.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are partially known from the detailed use-case requirements submitted before the 10 June clarification call and from the discovery conversation, but not yet formalized in an RFP or scoring rubric. The buyer is evaluating on: (1) Indirect procurement coverage (confirmed in-scope: IT, services, Capex indirect; direct is secondary/out-of-scope per 10 June — 'Zycus solution typically focuses more on the indirect side'); (2) Spend visibility and analytics to surface historical pricing, contract status, duplicate vendors, tail spend, and savings opportunities (buyer is 'working blindsided' without this data); (3) Supplier onboarding automation with fuzzy deduplication (PAN, GST) and self-service portal (current process is manual, siloed, email-based with multiple handoffs); (4) End-to-end RFx lifecycle with side-by-side bid comparison, tax/freight/terms normalization, and AI-driven award recommendation; (5) Proactive budget control at PR creation (SAP as single source of truth) to prevent completing sourcing without available budget (stated as a current failure mode); (6) Fast article/material code creation (<24h vs current 7-10 days); (7) Cross-plant stock visibility for internal transfer before external purchase (feasibility TBD, Zycus committed to check retail client implementations); (8) PO amendment with full version control and audit trail; (9) 3-way invoice match automation (currently manual, email-based invoices, MSME processing required); (10) Conversational AI embedded in Microsoft Teams for adoption/change management (Zycus demoed Merlin intake agent on 20 May, buyer responded positively: 'good to know that'). Implementation speed is a major criterion: buyer stated 'implementation should be this year itself... how fast can the implementation happen' and wants to 'take a decision very very soon.' Differentiation wedge per 20 May call: Zycus positioned organic end-to-end S2P suite on single codebase (vs competitors' bolt-on acquisitions), AI-native platform (AI since 2000, agentic AI first-to-market), and Teams-based conversational interface. Buyer acknowledged he 'knows how Ariba works, GEP, iValua' from prior hands-on implementations, so credibility bar is high. Known gap flagged 23 June: 'some of the requirements we cant deliver, will need clarity on that call' (3 July demo). If Zycus cannot deliver or offer a credible workaround, this becomes a decision-criteria blocker.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI appetite is implicit but not yet explicit. Buyer has not stated 'we want AI' or 'AI is a decision criterion,' but the pain points Utchal articulated (manual transactions consuming 50-60% of team time, change management/adoption challenges, desire for proactive automation) are classic AI use cases, and he responded positively to the Merlin Teams demo ('good to know that'). The detailed use-case requirements (proactive budget checks, fuzzy supplier deduplication, side-by-side bid comparison with AI-driven award recommendation, automated PO creation, 3-way invoice match automation) are all areas where AI/ML adds value. However, buyer has not yet framed AI as a requirement or wedge. Tier: AI Curious (implicit appetite, positive reception to AI demo, but not yet an explicit decision criterion). On 3 July, Zycus must make AI explicit: position Merlin as the adoption/time-savings engine that solves the 50-60% manual-transaction pain, and tie it to the conservative 4-5% savings target (AI finds the leakage, automates the follow-ups, accelerates the cycle).

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Customer expectations are well-defined from discovery (20 May) and clarification (10 June) calls: indirect procurement automation (spend analytics, supplier onboarding, sourcing, PR-to-PO, invoice automation), proactive budget checks, 24h article-code creation, cross-plant stock visibility, Teams-based conversational AI, fast implementation ('this year itself'). Zycus is aligned on most requirements (spend analytics is Zycus strength, supplier onboarding/RFx/CLM/invoice automation are core modules, SAP integration is proven with 67% customer base, Merlin Teams agent is differentiated). Two areas of exposure: (1) Functional gap acknowledged 23 June ('some of the requirements we cant deliver, will need clarity on that call') — unspecified, must be resolved on 3 July demo or buyer may lean toward Ariba/GEP; (2) Cross-plant stock visibility for internal transfer before external buy — feasibility unclear, Zycus committed to check retail client implementations but no commitment yet. If Zycus cannot deliver on these two, fit is partially exposed.
